comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is.  The justification for this is the emission factor is based on sampling which did not detect the pollutant in any of the sampling runs used to develop the factor.  Thus, the emission factor used represents a factor based on one-half the of the detection limit.  Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).

comments.comment: Water - Metal concentrations occur in the natural environment and these concentrations fluctuate and can vary between samples.  The concentration of the metal in the water from and in the water released to the North Saskatchewan River is based on  grab samples.  The concentrations are within Alberta Environment permitted and regulated levels of effluent concentrations. The methodology used to determine the load assumes a static concentration of intake and effluent water throughout the year, so the calculated load is largely dependent on the annual flow of water into, and out of, the facility. type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
Hydrochloric Acid

As noted within the comments, the calculation of Hydrochloric Acid was updated to incorporate a more up to date emission factor.  Prior to 2012 the emission factor used was 0.007 kg/tonne of coal and it was based on stack testing done on January 13, 1999. Since this data is over 10 years old it is deemed as noncurrent.  Therefore, we have now applied the emission factor from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1, Table 1.1-15 of 1.2 (Ib/ton) (which converts to 0.6 kg/tonne of coal) to our calculation.

We have noticed that we mistakenly switched the Hydrogen Fluoride emission factor with the Hydrochloric Acid emission factor within our calculation spreadsheets.  We will update the online submission.


Keephills 3 (stack 2) was successfully commissioned on September 1, 2011 and added to the total substance amounts from then on.

Updated emission factor  to source from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
Hydrogen Fluoride

As noted within the comments, the calculation of Hydrogen Fluoride was updated to incorporate a more up to date emission factor.  Prior to 2012 the emission factor used was 0.041 kg/tonne of coal and it was based on stack testing done on January 13, 1999. Since this data is over 10 years old it is deemed as noncurrent.  Therefore, we have now applied the emission factor from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1, Table 1.1-15 of 0.15 (Ib/ton) (which converts to 0.075 kg/tonne of coal) to our calculation.

We have noticed that we mistakenly switched the Hydrogen Fluoride emission factor with the Hydrochloric Acid emission factor within our calculation spreadsheets.  We will update the online submission.



Keephills 3 (stack 2) was successfully commissioned on September 1, 2011 and added to the total substance amounts from then on.

Updated emission factor to source from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1

comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is. Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).

type.fr: Commentaire sur le statut de déclaration de la substance

comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is.  The justification for this is the emission factor is based on sampling which did not detect the pollutant in any of the sampling runs used to develop the factor.  Thus, the emission factor used represents a factor based on one-half the of the detection limit.  Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Stack tests are done to determine the amount of metals released in air emissions. Based on the latest stack tests, the amount of lead in air emissions has dropped significantly. This may be attributable to the low amounts of coal being burnt at the facility with more natural gas being used in the fuel mix in 2020.

type.fr: Commentaire sur le recyclage

comments.comment: Batteries used at site and transferred offsite for recycling contain lead. With a variation in the number of batteries being sent for recycling each year, a variation in the lead emissions associated is expected.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Keephills has a mercury capture system (activated carbon injection) which removes mercury from stack exhaust gases. In 2020, this capture system was active for a shorter period of time compared to 2019 due to operational changes and the units being turned on and off more frequently. As a result, despite less production and less coal being burnt in 2020, the mercury air emissions increased overall.

type.fr: Commentaire sur l'élimination

comments.comment: With production in 2020 being lower than 2019, the use of fuels also dropped proportionally. This drop in fuel use as well as an increase of natural gas in the fuel blend led to a decrease in mercury emissions from ash disposed onsite as less ash was produced overall.

type.fr: Commentaire sur le recyclage

type.fr: Commentaire sur le statut de déclaration de la substance

comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is.  The justification for this is the emission factor is based on sampling which did not detect the pollutant in any of the sampling runs used to develop the factor.  Thus, the emission factor used represents a factor based on one-half the of the detection limit.  Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Keephills has a mercury capture system (activated carbon injection) which removes mercury from stack exhaust gases. There is a 12% decrease in mercury air releases due to the mercury capture system and less coal combustion with co-firing.

type.fr: Commentaire sur l'élimination

comments.comment: Onsite disposal consists of fly ash and bottom ash collected from the boilers. There is a significant reduction of the metals including mercury to the land disposal due to less amount coal being used for power generation and conversion from coal to natural gas generation systems.

type.fr: Commentaire sur le recyclage

comments.comment: This recycled amount constitutes ash that was sold offsite to cement companies for their manufacturing processes. With the variations in the amount of ash bought by companies on an as-needed basis, this variation in quantity recycled is expected. In 2021, the coal firing came to an end with the coal phase-out. This led to the removal of the remaining ashes from the bottom of the boilers. Two mercury bulbs were also sent to GFL Environment for recycling.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Arsenic emissions from natural gas combustion changed only 1.4% compared to 2022. The water emissions due to blowdown led to a large jump in emissions this year as the Keephills facility had not been blowing down for a few years, and the arsenic levels in its cooling pond had increased due to low water circulation. This is a one time thing and with the cooling pond being regularly replenished with water from the NSR now, the arsenic levels are expected to reduce overtime.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
The switch from coal to natural gas for electricity production, combined with lower overall production in 2022 led to a large drop in SO2 emissions. Coal was a significant source of sulphur dioxide emissions due to to its sulphur content, as such the switch to natural gas led to a huge reduction in these emissions. 
When the facility used coal as fuel, sulphur dioxide emissions were measured using continuous monitoring systems (CEMS). Since the phase off of coal, this data is no longer collected. Thus published emission factors were used to calculate SO2 emissions in 2022.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Hydrochloric acid emissions from stacks used to occur as a result of coal burn for electricity production. The facility phased off coal at the end of 2021 and only burned natural gas for electricity production in 2022. Natural gas combustion for electricity production does not produce HCL emissions (in a quantifiable quantity).

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Hydrogen fluoride emissions used to be a result of coal burn at the facility for electricity production. Since the phase off coal at the end of 2021, the facility only used natural gas for electricity production, which is not a source of HF emissions. HF emissions will no longer be reported under NPRI 2023 onwards.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Cadmium emissions were previously based on annual SES stack test results. Since the phase off coal, these tests are no longer conducted and past data is not representative of current plant operating conditions (as natural gas is burnt instead). As such, EPA's published emission factors for natural gas combustion were used. This led to an increase in emissions compared to 2021.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Cyanide emissions were previously reported due to the link of cyanide with coal combustion. Since the phase off of coal, cyanide will no longer be reported and will be removed from NPRI 2022 onwards.

comments.comment:
Mercury was a major emission source when the facility was using coal for electricity combustion. Since the phase off coal in December 2021, the facility has been using natural gas only which is a much lower source of mercury emissions. This led to a large drop in mercury emissions in 2022. 
While the facility was burning coal mercury emissions were measuring using continuous monitoring systems (CEMS). This data is no longer collected after the switch to natural gas, thus published emission factors were used to estimate mercury emissions for 2022.
